Shashwat Satapathy.strength: Explains the management-contract concept clearly using relatable analogies (comparing to Accor/Ginger-style channel management) and gives accurate technical specifics for Olive's land/room requirements.
Shashwat Satapathy.risk: Cited an unverifiable occupancy statistic (85% vs. industry 65%) not found in any brand material, and one call ended when the prospect denied ever registering interest, pointing to a lead-quality gap.
Shashwat Satapathy.insight: In calls since yesterday, Shashwat showed solid technical fluency on Olive's land/FSI requirements and the Spark rollout numbers, but leaned on an unsupported occupancy claim, and one lead turned out to be a false match.

Vikas S.strength: Strong follow-up discipline — references exact prior-call details (property size, location, promises) and sets concrete callback times.
Vikas S.risk: Several calls stayed in a holding pattern without a next milestone, and brand identity was occasionally muddled — introducing himself as 'from Olive Hotels' while discussing Spark-specific requirements.
Vikas S.insight: In calls since yesterday, Vikas correctly quoted Spark by Hilton's 60-room minimum in one call (matching the brand book) and showed strong relationship continuity, but sometimes blurred which brand he was representing on the open.

Sukhpreet Singh Lohia.strength: Accurate, concise brand introduction that correctly links Embassy Group, Olive, and Spark by Hilton in the right hierarchy, and stayed polite and professional even when the prospect was disengaged.
Sukhpreet Singh Lohia.risk: No discovery or objection-handling could be observed in this window — both calls ended within the first 45-75 seconds before any qualifying questions were asked, so pitch depth and follow-through remain untested on recent evidence.
Sukhpreet Singh Lohia.insight: In calls since yesterday, Lohia opened both calls with a clean, accurate brand chain (Embassy Group to Olive to Spark by Hilton) but neither call progressed past the initial framing — one prospect showed no interest and the other never picked up.

Prasoon Singh.strength: Persisted with discovery questions (room count, location, readiness) even when prospects gave short or evasive answers, and handled a flat 'not in the hotel industry' rejection calmly.
Prasoon Singh.risk: Pitch structure and clarity are weak — multiple calls lack a clean opening/value-prop sequence and drift into confusing back-and-forth, which likely undermines prospect confidence early in the conversation.
Prasoon Singh.insight: In calls since yesterday, Prasoon Singh kept attempting to gather basic property details (room count, location) across all four calls, but the pitch delivery itself was frequently fragmented and difficult to follow, with several exchanges reading as disjointed half-sentences rather than a structured conversation.

Haresh Kumar.strength: Polite, patient opening; checks for a good time to talk before pitching.
Haresh Kumar.risk: Only 2 of 7 new calls reached any brand-specific content; the rest were voicemails/pushback/callback requests, and the intro consistently defaults to a generic 'Hilton Group of Hotels' tag.
Haresh Kumar.insight: In calls since yesterday, Haresh opened nearly every call with a generic 'Hilton Group of Hotels' introduction rather than naming and explaining Spark by Hilton or Olive by Embassy; most calls ended within 30-140 seconds with no substantive pitch.

Ashish Varma.strength: Handles a high volume of cold outbound calls efficiently and disqualifies undersized properties (7-15 rooms) quickly and politely without wasting time.
Ashish Varma.risk: Gave inconsistent minimum room-count figures across calls in the same window (30 rooms in most, but 50 in at least one Olive conversation), which could confuse prospects on brand fit.
Ashish Varma.insight: In calls since yesterday, Ashish moved through a large volume of dials with disciplined, polite disqualification of undersized properties, but the room-count mixup and several very short (30-75s) low-yield calls suggest some script fatigue rather than deepening the conversation.

Iqura Hussain.strength: On the fuller call, asked the right qualifying questions (key count, room size, furnishing/readiness status) and made a sound, honest brand-fit recommendation (Open management vs. re-branding to Olive) rather than a generic pitch.
Iqura Hussain.risk: The newest call (2026-07-07) is very thin — under a minute, no discovery questions asked, ends in a vague 'I'll connect you with another contact' deferral — a pattern that, if it continues, would leave too little signal to assess consistently.
Iqura Hussain.insight: In calls since yesterday, Iqura Hussain showed good product-fit judgment on a substantive call (correctly matching an already-furnished, ready property to the Open Hotels model rather than forcing an Olive conversion) but the second/newest call was thin and rushed, ending in an immediate handoff with almost no discovery.

Shiva Srivastava.strength: Persistent in re-engaging prospects and consistently asks for WhatsApp/location details to keep leads alive.
Shiva Srivastava.risk: Brand story is essentially absent from the open ('Hilton' used as a bare tag), and minimum-room figures swing between 50 and 30 across calls with no consistent rule for which brand each belongs to.
Shiva Srivastava.insight: In calls since yesterday, Shiva opened nearly every call with a bare 'Hilton'/'Hilton Hotel' tag rather than the premium-economy positioning story, and gave inconsistent minimum-room figures across the window.

Mohd Zaib.strength: Consistently explains the premium-economy category, average room rent band (Rs 4,000-8,000), and management-agreement structure in his own words, and correctly avoids calling Spark a 4-star product.
Mohd Zaib.risk: The 'Spark minimum 50 rooms' claim recurs across many calls (6+ of 18 in the 24h window) without correction, and several calls end abruptly with no confirmed next step.
Mohd Zaib.insight: In calls since yesterday, Zaib delivered a fairly complete Spark pitch across most calls but kept repeating a flat 50-room minimum for Spark by Hilton (actual range is 60-150 keys), once conflating it with Olive's lower threshold.

Inderjeet Ahlawat.strength: On his one substantive call he ran a genuinely consultative conversation — probing land status, CLU, acreage, and flexibly proposing a 30-room configuration when the prospect couldn't meet a larger ask.
Inderjeet Ahlawat.risk: Repeats the flagged 'Spark by Hilton minimum 50 rooms' error (book states 60-150 keys) across multiple calls despite this being called out previously, and opens most calls with a generic 'Embassy Hilton' label rather than the distinct Spark story.
Inderjeet Ahlawat.insight: In calls since yesterday, Inderjeet showed strong discovery depth and negotiation flexibility in his longest call, but continued misstating Spark's room minimum as 50, and most other calls in the window were short, low-engagement dials or voicemails with little discovery beyond property location.

Gourang Patil.strength: On calls where the prospect engages, delivers a clear, accurate Olive-vs-Spark distinction and cites the correct AI-driven staffing-efficiency stat, backed by structured discovery on location, demand generators, and project stage.
Gourang Patil.risk: A meaningful share of calls in the window yield almost no information exchange (wrong numbers, quick disconnects), and on one call the company name came out garbled as 'MLC Group' instead of Embassy.
Gourang Patil.insight: In calls since yesterday, Gourang's engaged calls were strong and factually sound — correct brand differentiation and solid qualifying questions — but roughly half the dials in the window were short, low-yield, or misdirected.

Syed Mazher Shah.strength: Solid lead-screening judgment: correctly flagged a 24-room property as too small and routed it to a regional colleague rather than overselling; also correctly declined a resort-type vacant-land lead that didn't fit the management-contract model.
Syed Mazher Shah.risk: Communication clarity is inconsistent — one call (Call 2) was largely incomprehensible due to rapid language-switching and cross-talk, and in a voicemail message the flagship brand was referred to as 'Park Hotels by Hilton' rather than Spark by Hilton, a naming slip worth watching.
Syed Mazher Shah.insight: In calls since yesterday, Shah consistently qualified leads on room count and location before engaging further, but delivery clarity varied sharply between calls — some conversations were coherent and professional, others degenerated into hard-to-follow code-switching between Hindi, Telugu and English.

Arpit Shukla.strength: Clearly separated Spark by Hilton and Olive as two distinct offerings and proposed a sensible next step — sending detailed collateral for both, with a colleague following up.
Arpit Shukla.risk: Stated Spark's minimum room requirement as 50, which understates the brand book's actual 60-150 key range — the same recurring inaccuracy seen across other BDs.
Arpit Shukla.insight: In the one call recorded since yesterday, Arpit ran good structured discovery (room count, banquet capacity, expansion plans, IFSI status) and offered the prospect two clear paths forward, though the sample is a single call so this read is limited, and he repeated the known Spark room-minimum error.

Ajith KS.strength: Strong discovery discipline: systematically asked about room count, construction status, and expansion scope, and set transparent expectations early (e.g., minimum room thresholds) rather than overpromising.
Ajith KS.risk: Cited aggregate figures (e.g., '54 hotels across Bangalore') and applied a '10-year' term framing to a general Olive conversation rather than specifically to Spark by Hilton (where the 10-year term is documented) — worth double-checking against source figures for accuracy.
Ajith KS.insight: In calls since yesterday, Ajith KS ran the most structured and articulate calls in this batch — clear opening, explicit qualifying questions, and transparent expectation-setting — but a couple of brand statistics cited to prospects didn't clearly line up with documented figures.
